Home.tagline: Stop cramming prompts. Click visual tags to build pro prompts for AI image and video — Nano Banana, GPT-image-2, Midjourney, FLUX, Seedance, Veo, Kling, and more.

About.title: About IMGPrompt
About.intro1: IMGPrompt is a free, open-source visual prompt builder for AI image and video models, including Nano Banana, GPT-image-2, Midjourney, FLUX, Seedance, Veo, and Kling (plus Stable Diffusion and DALL·E via compatible syntax). Through visual tag selection with bilingual (English + your language) labels, you can assemble high-quality English prompts without memorizing every keyword.
About.intro2: The tool runs entirely in your browser — no backend database, no uploads of your creative work. No signup, no payment, and fully open-source for self-hosting or auditing. Desktop clients for Windows, macOS, and Linux are also available, with full offline capability.

HowTo.title: Generate Professional Prompts in Three Steps

step1.title: Pick a Subject
step1.desc: Choose what you want to depict from the subject list — people, animals, landscapes, architecture, concept art, and so on. This scopes the tags available in the following steps.

step2.title: Select a Category
step2.desc: Drill down into a sub-category under your subject, e.g. hairstyles, clothing, poses, or expressions under "people". Categories help you cover every dimension of your prompt systematically.

step3.title: Choose Tags and Generate
step3.desc: Click a tag to insert it into the prompt box; click again to remove. The right panel assembles the result live — copy with one click, append lighting or quality boosters, or add negative prompts before exporting.

Terminology.title: AI Prompt Terminology
Terminology.prompt: Prompt. A natural-language description of the image you want to generate — the core input for text-to-image models.
Terminology.negative: Negative Prompt. A list of elements you want to exclude from the image, such as lowres, blurry, bad hands, or watermark, used to improve output quality.
Terminology.weight: Weight. Adjusts keyword emphasis using parentheses or numeric values, e.g. (masterpiece:1.2) makes the word more influential; higher values mean stronger effect.
Terminology.sampler: Sampler. Determines the path the model takes from noise to image — common choices include DPM++ 2M Karras, Euler a, and UniPC, each with different speed and detail trade-offs.
Terminology.seed: Seed. Controls the random starting state; with a fixed seed and unchanged parameters, the same prompt reproduces the same image — useful for debugging and fine-tuning.
Terminology.cfg: CFG Scale (Classifier-Free Guidance). Higher values make the model follow your prompt more strictly; typical range is 7–12. Too high causes over-fitting and a stiff, posterized look.
Terminology.style: Style. Keywords describing overall artistic direction — anime, photorealistic, oil painting, cyberpunk — usually placed near the start of the prompt.

q1.question: What is IMGPrompt? Which AI art models does it support?
q1.answer: IMGPrompt is a prompt editor for text-to-image models that helps you compose English prompts through visual tags with bilingual labels. It officially supports Nano Banana, GPT-image-2, Midjourney, FLUX, Seedance, Veo, and Kling for image and video; the generated prompts also work with Stable Diffusion, DALL·E, and any model that accepts compatible syntax.

q2.question: Is it free? Do I need to sign up? Is my data uploaded?
q2.answer: Completely free, no registration or login required. All prompt composition and input happens locally in your browser — nothing is uploaded to any server, and there is no backend database. The project is open-source on GitHub; you can audit the code or self-host.

q3.question: How long should a prompt be? Is there a character limit?
q3.answer: We recommend staying under 380 characters — this approximates the token limit of Stable Diffusion's default CLIP text encoder, beyond which tokens may be truncated or weakened. The tool shows a live character counter. Midjourney, GPT-image-2, and Nano Banana are more lenient on length, but concise prompts generally produce better results.

q4.question: What is the difference between positive and Negative Prompts? How do I use them?
q4.answer: Positive prompts describe what should appear (e.g. masterpiece, best quality, detailed face); negative prompts describe what to avoid (e.g. lowres, blurry, bad anatomy, extra fingers). The "Negative" button copies a curated set of common negative prompts to your clipboard — paste it into your model's Negative Prompt field.

q5.question: How do I translate prompts from my language into English?
q5.answer: The right panel includes a Translate button: enter text in any language, click Translate, and the tool calls a translation API to convert it to English and append it to the prompt box. Handy for starting from an inspirational sentence instead of looking up each word.

q6.question: How do weights work? Why do I see parentheses in prompts?
q6.answer: In Stable Diffusion WebUI syntax, (word) raises the weight to 1.1, ((word)) to 1.21, and so on; you can also write (word:1.3) to set it explicitly. Midjourney uses ::2 syntax for weights. IMGPrompt preserves the original English tokens so you can fine-tune weights per your target model's rules.
